content: The value of $${(1 - \omega + {\omega ^2})^5} + {(1 + \omega - {\omega ^2})^5}$$, where $$\omega$$ and $$\omega$$2 are the complex cube roots of unity is
Let $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of $${x^2} - 2x\cos \phi + 1 = 0$$, then the equation whose roots are $${\alpha ^n},{\beta ^n}$$ is
explanation:
$$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of $${x^2} - 2x\cos \phi + 1 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha + \beta = 2\cos \phi $$ ...... (i)

$$\alpha \beta = 1$$ ..... (ii)

$${(\alpha - \beta )^2} = {(\alpha + \beta )^2} - 4\alpha \beta = 4{\cos ^2}\phi - 4$$

$$ = 4({\cos ^2}\phi - 1) = 4{i^2}{\sin ^2}\phi $$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha - \beta = \pm \,2i\sin \phi $$ ..... (iii)

From (i) and (iii),

$$\alpha = \cos \phi \pm i\sin \phi $$

$$\beta = \cos \phi \mp i\sin \phi $$

$${\alpha ^n} + {\beta ^n} = \cos n\phi \pm i\sin n\phi + \cos n\phi \mp i\sin n\phi $$

$$ = 2\cos n\phi $$ ..... (iv)

$${\alpha ^n}{\beta ^n} = {(\alpha \beta )^n} = {(1)^n} = 1$$ ..... (v)

Equation whose roots are $${\alpha ^n},{\beta ^n}$$ is

$${x^2} - ({\alpha ^n} + {\beta ^n})x + {\alpha ^n}{\beta ^n} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - (2\cos n\phi )x + 1 = 0$$ [from (iv) and (v)]

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2x\cos n\phi + 1 = 0$$.

content: The principal amplitude of $${(\sin 40^\circ + i\cos 40^\circ )^5}$$ is
explanation:
$${(\sin 40^\circ + i\cos 40^\circ )^5} = {(\cos (90^\circ - 40^\circ ) + i\sin (90^\circ + 40^\circ ))^5}$$

$$ = {(\cos 50^\circ + i\sin 50^\circ )^5} = \cos 5 \times 50^\circ + i\sin 5 \times 50^\circ $$ ($$\because$$ $${(\cos \theta + i\sin \theta )^n} = \cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta ,\,n \in I)$$)

$$ = \cos 250^\circ + i\sin 250^\circ $$ ($$\because$$ $$250^\circ $$ lies in 3rd quadrant)

$$ = \cos (180^\circ + 70^\circ ) + i\sin (180^\circ + 70^\circ )$$

$$ = - \cos 70^\circ - i\sin 70^\circ $$

(principal amplitude $$\theta$$ of $$\cos \theta + i\sin \theta $$ lies in $$( - 180^\circ ,180^\circ )$$ here 250$$^\circ$$ lies in 3rd quadrant. $$\therefore$$ principal amplitude will lie in $$( - 180^\circ , - 90^\circ )$$

$$ = \cos (180^\circ - 70^\circ ) - i\sin (180^\circ - 70^\circ )$$

$$ = \cos 110^\circ - i\sin 110^\circ $$

$$ = \cos ( - 110^\circ ) + i\sin ( - 110^\circ )$$

$$\therefore$$ principal amplitude $$ = - 110^\circ $$.

content: A and B are two points on the Argand plane such that the segment AB is bisected at the point (0, 0). If the point A, which is in the third quadrant has principal amplitude $$\theta$$, then the principal amplitude of the point B is
explanation:
A lies in 3rd quadrant, let $$\theta$$ is amplitude of A

$$\therefore$$ $$-$$180$$^\circ$$ < $$\theta$$ < $$-$$90$$^\circ$$ (principal amplitude)

B will lie in Ist quadrant, so principal amplitude $$\phi$$ will lie in 0 < $$\phi$$ < 90$$^\circ$$.

From figure

$$-$$ $$\theta$$ + $$\phi$$ = 180$$^\circ$$

$$\phi$$ = 180$$^\circ$$ $$-$$ ($$-$$$$\theta$$) ($$\because$$ $$\theta$$ is $$-$$ ve)

= 180$$^\circ$$ + $$\theta$$ = $$\pi$$ + $$\theta$$

e.g. if $$\theta$$ = $$-$$120$$^\circ$$

then $$\phi$$ = 180$$^\circ$$ + ($$-$$120$$^\circ$$)

= 60$$^\circ$$ (principal amplitude of B).

content: For two complex numbers z1, z2 the relation $$\left| {{z_1} + {z_2}} \right| = \left| {{z_1}} \right| + \left| {{z_2}} \right|$$ holds if
explanation:
Let $${z_1} = {r_1}(\cos {\theta _1} + i\sin {\theta _1})$$ and $${z_2} = {r_2}(\cos {\theta _2} + i\sin {\theta _2})$$

$$\therefore$$ $${z_1} + {z_2} = ({r_1}\cos {\theta _1} + {r_2}\cos {\theta _2}) + i({r_1}\sin {\theta _1} + {r_2}\sin {\theta _2})$$

$$\left| {{z_1} + {z_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{({r_1}\cos {\theta _1} + {r_2}\cos {\theta _2})}^2} + {{({r_1}\sin {\theta _1} + {r_2}\sin {\theta _2})}^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {r_1^2 + r_2^2 + 2{r_1}{r_2}\cos ({\theta _1} - {\theta _2})} $$

$$\left| {{z_1}} \right| = {r_1},\,\left| {{z_2}} \right| = {r_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {r_1^2 + r_2^2 + 2{r_1}{r_2}\cos ({\theta _1} - {\theta _2})} = {r_1} + {r_2}$$ (given $$\left| {{z_1} + {z_2}} \right| = \left| {{z_1}} \right| + \left| {{z_2}} \right|$$)

(squaring both sides)

$$ \Rightarrow r_1^2 + r_2^2 + 2{r_1}{r_2}\cos ({\theta _1} - {\theta _2}) = r_1^2 + r_2^2 + 2{r_1}{r_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos ({\theta _1} - {\theta _2}) = \cos 0^\circ \Rightarrow {\theta _1} = {\theta _2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \arg ({z_1}) = \arg ({z_2})$$.

content: If 1, $$\omega$$, $$\omega$$2 are cube roots of unity, then $$\left| {\matrix{ 1 & {{\omega ^n}} & {{\omega ^{2n}}} \cr {{\omega ^{2n}}} & 1 & {{\omega ^n}} \cr {{\omega ^n}} & {{\omega ^{2n}}} & 1 \cr } } \right|$$ has value
explanation:
Property of $$\omega$$ is $$\omega$$3 = 1 and 1 + $$\omega$$ + $$\omega$$2 = 0

$$\therefore$$ $$\left| {\matrix{ 1 & {{\omega ^n}} & {{\omega ^{2n}}} \cr {{\omega ^{2n}}} & 1 & {{\omega ^n}} \cr {{\omega ^n}} & {{\omega ^{2n}}} & 1 \cr } } \right|$$

$$ = 1(1 - {\omega ^n} \times {\omega ^{2n}}) - {\omega ^n}({\omega ^{2n}} - {\omega ^n} \times {\omega ^n}) + {\omega ^{2n}}({\omega ^{2n}} \times {\omega ^{2n}} - {\omega ^n})$$

$$ = 1 - {\omega ^{3n}} - {\omega ^{3n}} + {\omega ^{3n}} + {\omega ^{6n}} - {\omega ^{3n}} = 1 - 2{({\omega ^3})^n} + {({\omega ^3})^{2n}}$$

$$ = 1 - 2{(1)^n} + {(1)^{2n}} = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0$$.

content: If $$i = \sqrt { - 1} $$ and n is positive integer, then $${i^n} + {i^{n + 1}} + {i^{n + 2}} + {i^{n + 3}}$$ is equal to
The modulus of $${{1 - i} \over {3 + i}} + {{4i} \over 5}$$ is
For any complex number z, the minimum value of $$|z| + |z - 1|$$ is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$||{z_1}| - |{z_2}|| \le |{z_1} - {z_2}|$$

$$ \Rightarrow ||z| - 1| \le |z - 1|$$

$$ \Rightarrow - |z - 1| \le |z| - 1| \le |z - 1|$$

$$ \Rightarrow - |z - 1| \le |z| - 1 \Rightarrow 1 \le |z| + |z - 1|$$

So minimum value of $$|z| + |z - 1|$$ is 1

content: If $$z = {4 \over {1 - i}}$$, then $$\overline z $$ is (where $$\overline z $$ is complex conjugate of z)
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$z = {4 \over {1 - i}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\overline z = {4 \over {1 + i}}$$

content: If $$ - \pi < \arg (z) < - {\pi \over 2}$$, then $$\arg \overline z - \arg ( - \overline z )$$ is
explanation: $$\arg (\overline z ) - \arg ( - \overline z ) = \arg \left( {{{\overline z } \over { - \overline z }}} \right) = \arg ( - 1) = \pi $$

content: For the real parameter t, the locus of the complex number $$z = (1 - {t^2}) + i\sqrt {1 + {t^2}} $$ in the complex plane is
explanation:
Let $$z = x + iy$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = 1 - {t^2}$$, $$y = \sqrt {1 + {t^2}} $$on eliminating t, we get $$x = 1 - ({y^2} - 1)$$

$$\therefore$$ $${y^2} = - x + 2$$, which is a parabola.

content: If $$x + {1 \over x} = 2\cos \theta $$, then for any integer n, $${x^n} + {1 \over {{x^n}}} = $$
explanation:
Let $$x = \cos \theta + i\sin \theta $$ as $$x + {1 \over x} = 2\cos \theta $$ is given.

$$\therefore$$ $${x^n} = \cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta $$ & $$1/{x^n} = \cos n\theta - i\sin n\theta $$ [From De Moivre's Theorem, as n = integer]

$$\therefore$$ $${x^n} + {1 \over {{x^n}}} = 2\cos n\theta $$.

content: If $$\omega$$ $$\ne$$ 1 is a cube root of unity, then the sum of the series $$S = 1 + 2\omega + 3{\omega ^2} + \,\,.....\,\, + 3n{\omega ^{3n - 1}}$$ is
If $z_1, z_2$ are complex numbers such that $\frac{2 z_1}{3 z_2}$ is a purely imaginary number, then the value of $\left|\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_1+z_2}\right|$ is
explanation:
We are given that $z_1$ and $z_2$ are complex numbers such that $\frac{2 z_1}{3 z_2}$ is a purely imaginary number.

Let $\frac{2 z_1}{3 z_2}=k i$, where $k$ as a non-zero real number.

Then, $2 z_1=3 z_2 k i$.

$$ \frac{z_1}{z_2}=\frac{3}{2} k i . $$

Now, consider the expansion $\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_1+z_2}$.

Divide the numerator and denominator by $z_2$.

$$ \frac{\frac{z_1}{z_2}-1}{\frac{z_1}{z_2}+1}=\frac{\frac{3}{2} k i-1}{\frac{3}{2} k i+1} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore\left|\frac{-1+\frac{3}{2} k i}{1+\frac{3}{2} k i}\right| & =\frac{\sqrt{(-1)^2+\left(\frac{3}{2} k\right)^2}}{\sqrt{1^2+\left(\frac{3}{2} k\right)^2}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{1+\frac{9}{4} k^2}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{9}{4} k^2}}=1 \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $\omega(\neq 1)$ be a cubic root of unity. Then the minimum value of the set $\left\{\mid a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right\}^2 ; a, b, c$ are distinct non-zero integers} equals
explanation:
Let $\omega$ be a cubic root of unity $(\omega \neq 1)$.

We need to find the minimum value of $\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|^2$, where $a, b, c$ are distinct non-zero integers.

We know that

$$ \left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|^2=a^2+b^2+c^2-a b-b c-c a $$

This expression can be rewritten as

$$ \frac{1}{2}\left((a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2\right) $$

The smallest possible absolute difference between two distinct integers is 1 .

Consider consecutive integers, for example,

$$ a=1, b=2, c=3 . $$

$$ \begin{gathered}Then\,\, (a-b)^2-(1-2)^2=(-1)^2=1 \\ (b-c)^2=(2-3)^2=(-1)^2=1 \end{gathered} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad(c-a)^2=(3-1)^2=(2)^2=4 $$

The sum of squares of differences is $1+1+4=6$ The minimum value of $\frac{1}{2}(6)=3$.

content: If $\left|Z_1\right|=\left|Z_2\right|=\left|Z_3\right|=1$ and $Z_1+Z_2+Z_3=0$, then the area of the triangle whose vertices are $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$ is
explanation:
Given, $\left|Z_1\right|=\left|Z_2\right|=\left|Z_3\right|=1$

and  $$ Z_1+Z_2+Z_3=0 $$

This condition implies that $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$ form an equilateral triangle inscribed in the unit circle centered at the origin.

The side length of an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle of radius $R$ is $R \sqrt{3}$. Here $R=1$, so, the side length $s=\sqrt{3}$.

The area of an equilateral triangle with side lengths $s$ is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} s^2$.

$$ \text { Area }=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(\sqrt{3})^2=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 3=\frac{3 \sqrt{4}}{4} $$

content: If $$z_1$$ and $$z_2$$ be two roots of the equation $$z^2+a z+b=0, a^2<4 b$$, then the origin, $$\mathrm{z}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{z}_2$$ form an equilateral triangle if

$$a_0 x^n+a_1 x^{n-1}+\ldots .+a_{n-1} x+a_n=0, a_0, a_1, \ldots . a_n \in \mathbb{R}, a_0 \neq 0,$$

then the value of $$a_1 \sin \theta+a_2 \sin 2 \theta+\ldots .+a_n \sin n \theta$$ is
If the vertices of a square are $${z_1},{z_2},{z_3}$$ and $${z_4}$$ taken in the anti-clockwise order, then $${z_3} = $$
explanation:
At A, using rotation theorem,

$$\frac{z_3-z_2}{z_1-z_2}=\frac{B A}{D A} e^{-i\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}$$

Since, it is a square, $$A B=A D$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \frac{z_3-z_2}{z_1-z_2} & =\cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=-i \\ \Rightarrow & & z_3-z_2 & =-i\left(z_1-z_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & z_3 & =-i z_1+(1+i) z_2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Reflection of the line $$\overline a z + a\overline z = 0$$ in the real axis is given by :
explanation:
Reflection of $$\bar{a} z+a \bar{z}=0$$ in real axis is obtained by transforming $$Z$$ to $$\bar{z}$$

So, required line is $$\bar{a} Z+a(Z)=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \bar{a} z+a z=0$$

explanation:

explanation:
Let z = ei$$\theta$$

Also, let $$w = {{{e^{i\theta }}} \over {1 - {e^{2i\theta }}}} = {1 \over {{e^{ - i\theta }} - {e^{i\theta }}}}$$

content: Let C denote the set of all complex numbers. Define A = {(z, w) | z, w$$\in$$C and |z| = |w|}, B = {z, w} | z, w$$\in$$C and z<sup>2</sup> = w<sup>2</sup>}. Then
explanation:
We have, z2 = w2

$$\Rightarrow$$ z2 $$-$$ w2 = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ (z $$-$$ w) (z + w) = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ z = w, z = $$-$$w

$$\Rightarrow$$ | z | = | w |

$$\therefore$$ B $$\subset$$ A

content: The number of complex numbers p such that $$\left| p \right| = 1$$ and imaginary part of p<sup>4</sup> is 0, is
explanation:
Let complex number

$$P = \cos \theta + i\sin \theta $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\left| P \right| = 1$$ and $$\theta \in \left[ {0,2\pi } \right)$$

Now, $${P^4} = {(\cos \theta + i\sin \theta )^4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {P^4} = \cos 4\theta + i\sin 4\theta $$

Imaginary part of P4 = 0

$$ \therefore $$ sin 4$$\theta $$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow 4\theta = n\pi \Rightarrow \theta = {\pi \over 4}$$

$$0 \le \theta \le 2\pi $$

= $$0 \le x < 8$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ n = 8

content: The equation $$z\bar z + (2 - 3i)z + (2 + 3i)\bar z + 4 = 0$$ represents a circle of radius
explanation:
Given,

$$z\bar z + (2 - 3i)z + (2 + 3i)\bar z + 4 = 0$$

This is the form of

$$z\bar z + \bar az + a\bar z + b$$

Here, a = 2 + 3i, b = 4

$$ \therefore $$ Radius of circle = $$\sqrt {{{\left| a \right|}^2} - b} $$

= $$\sqrt {(4 + 9) - 4} $$

= $$\sqrt {13 - 4} = 3$$

content: Let z be a complex number such that the principal value of argument, arg z > 0. Then, arg z $$-$$ arg($$-$$ z) is
explanation: $$ \therefore $$ arg(z) $$-$$ arg($$-$$ z) = $$\theta$$ $$-$$ ($$-$$ $$\pi$$ + $$\theta$$) = $$\pi$$

content: The general value of the real angle $$\theta$$, which satisfies the equation, $$(\cos \theta  + i\sin \theta )(\cos 2\theta  + i\sin 2\theta )...(\cos n\theta  + i\sin n\theta ) = 1$$ is given by, (assuming k is an integer)
explanation:
We have,

$$(\cos \theta + i\sin \theta )(\cos 2\theta + i\sin 2\theta )...(\cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta ) = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{i\theta }}\,.\,{e^{i(2\theta )}}\,.\,{e^{i(3\theta )}}\,.\,...\,{e^{i(n\theta )}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{i\theta (1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n)}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{{{i\,n(n + 1)\theta } \over 1}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \left( {{{n(n + 1)} \over 2}\theta } \right) + i\sin \left( {{{n(n + 1)} \over 2}} \right)\theta = 1 + 0i$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \left( {{{n(n + 1)} \over 2}\theta } \right) = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{n(n + 1)} \over 2}\theta = 2k\pi $$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {{4k} \over {n(n + 1)}}\pi $$

explanation:
Since, Q is the point for which PQ is bisected perpendicularly by the initial line (X-axis). Therefore, Q will be the image of P in X-axis

content: If $${Z_r} = \sin {{2\pi r} \over {11}} - i\cos {{2\pi r} \over {11}}$$, then $$\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{10} {{Z_r}} $$ is equal to
explanation:
We have, $${Z_r} = \sin {{2\pi r} \over {11}} - i\cos {{2\pi r} \over {11}}$$

$$ = - i\left( {\cos {{2\pi r} \over {11}} + i\sin {{2\pi r} \over {11}}} \right)$$

$$ = - i{e^{{{i2\pi r} \over {11}}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{10} {{Z_r}} = - i\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{10} {{e^{^{{{i2\pi r} \over {11}}}}}} $$

$$ = - i \times 0 = 0$$

content: If z<sub>1</sub> and z<sub>2</sub> be two non-zero complex numbers such that $${{{z_1}} \over {{z_2}}} + {{{z_2}} \over {{z_1}}} = 1$$, then the origin and the points represented by z<sub>1</sub> and z<sub>2</sub>
explanation:
We know that, if z1, z2 and z3 are the vertices of an equilateral triangle. Then,

$$z_1^2 + z_2^2 + z_3^2 - {z_1}{z_2} - {z_2}{z_3} - {z_3}{z_1} = 0$$ .... (i)

Now, but we have

$${{{z_1}} \over {{z_2}}} + {{{z_2}} \over {{z_1}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow z_1^2 + z_2^2 = {z_1}{z_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow z_1^2 + z_2^2 - {z_1}{z_2} = 0$$

Here, $${z_3} = 0$$

Hence, given points form an equilateral triangle.

content: Let z<sub>1</sub> and z<sub>2</sub> be complex numbers such that z<sub>1</sub> $$ \ne $$ z<sub>2</sub> and |z<sub>1</sub>| = |z<sub>2</sub>|. If Re(z<sub>1</sub>) > 0 and Im(z<sub>2</sub>) < 0, then $${{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}$$ is
explanation:
Let z1 = x1 + iy1 and z2 = x2 + iy2

Re(z1) > 0 $$ \Rightarrow $$ x1 > 0

and Im(z2) < 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y2 < 0

Given |z1| = |z2|

$$ \Rightarrow $$ |z1|2 = |z2|2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ z1$${\overline z _1}$$ = z2$${\overline z _2}$$

Now, $$\left( {{{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}} \right) + \left( {{{\overline {{z_1} + {z_2}} } \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}} \right)$$

= $$\left( {{{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}} \right) + \left( {{{{{\overline z }_1} + {{\overline z }_2}} \over {{{\overline z }_1} - {{\overline z }_2}}}} \right)$$

= $$\left( {{{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}} \right) + \left( {{{{{\overline z }_1} + {{\overline z }_2}} \over {{{\overline z }_1} - {{\overline z }_2}}}} \right)$$

$$ = {{{z_1}{{\overline z }_1} + {z_2}{{\overline z }_1} - {z_1}{{\overline z }_2} - {z_2}{{\overline z }_2} + {z_1}{{\overline z }_1} + {z_2}{{\overline z }_2} - {z_2}{{\overline z }_1} + {z_2}{{\overline z }_2}} \over {({z_1} - {z_2})({{\overline z }_1} - {{\overline z }_2})}}$$

$$ = {{2(|{z_1}{|^2}\, - \,|z{|^2})} \over {({z_1} - {z_2})({{\overline z }_1} - {{\overline z }_2})}} = 0$$ ($$ \because $$ $$|{\overline z _1}{|^2} = |{\overline z _2}{|^2}$$)

$$ = {{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}$$ is purely imaginary.

content: The expression $${{{{(1 + i)}^n}} \over {{{(1 - i)}^{n - 2}}}}$$ equals
Let z = x + iy, where x and y are real. The points (x, y) in the X-Y plane for which $${{{z + i} \over {z - i}}}$$ is purely imaginary, lie on
The value of $$\sum\limits_{n = 1}^{13} {({i^n} + {i^{n + 1}})} $$, $$i = \sqrt { - 1} $$ is

If $$\omega$$ is an imaginary cube root of unity, then the value of (2 $$-$$ $$\omega$$) (2 $$-$$ $$\omega$$<sup>2</sup>) + 2(3 $$-$$ $$\omega$$)(3 $$-$$ $$\omega$$<sup>2</sup>) + ... + (n $$-$$ 1) (n $$-$$ $$\omega$$)(n $$-$$ $$\omega$$<sup>2</sup>) is

Prove that if the ratio $${{z - i} \over {z - 1}}$$ is purely imaginary, the point z lies on the circle in the Argand plane whose centre is at the point $${1 \over 2}(1 + i)$$ and radius is $${1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$.
explanation:
Let z = x + iy, where x and y are both real

$$\therefore$$ $${{z - i} \over {z - 1}} = {{x + iy - i} \over {x + iy - 1}} = {{x + i(y - 1)} \over {(x - 1) + iy}}$$

$$ = {{[x + i(y - 1)][(x - 1) - iy]} \over {[(x - 1) + iy][(x - 1) - iy]}}$$ [by rationalisation]

$$ = {{({x^2} + {y^2} - x - y) + i(1 - x - y)} \over {{{(x - 1)}^2} + {y^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{x^2} + {y^2} - x - y} \over {{{(x - 1)}^2} + {y^2}}} + i{{1 - x - y} \over {{{(x - 1)}^2} + {y^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ If $${{z - i} \over {z - 1}}$$ is purely imaginary, its real part is zero.

$$\therefore$$ $${x^2} + {y^2} - x - y = 0$$

or

$$\left[ {{x^2} - 2\,.\,x\,.\,{1 \over 2} + {{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \right] + \left[ {{y^2} - 2\,.\,y\,.\,{1 \over 2} + {{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \right] = {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2}$$

[Adding (1/2)2 + (1/2)2 to both sides and rearranging]

$${\left( {x - {1 \over 2}} \right)^2} + {\left( {y - {1 \over 2}} \right)^2} = {1 \over 2} = {\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2}$$

This is the equation of a circle in the cartesian form with its centre $$\left( {{1 \over 2},{1 \over 2}} \right)$$ and radius $${1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$.

Hence, centre is at $${1 \over 2} + i{1 \over 2}$$, i.e., at $${{1 + i} \over 2}$$ in the Argand plane and its radius is $${1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$.

content: If z$$_1$$ and z$$_2$$ are two complex numbers satisfying the equation $$\left| {{{{z_1} + {z_2}} \over {{z_1} - {z_2}}}} \right| = 1$$, then $${{{z_1}} \over {{z_2}}}$$ may be
explanation:
We have, $$\left|\frac{z_1+z_2}{z_1-z_2}\right|=1$$

$$\text { Let } z_1=a+i b \text { and } z_2=c+i d$$

$$\left|\frac{(a+c)+i(b+d)}{(a-c)+i(b-d)}\right|=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad|(a+c)+i(b+d)|=|(a-c)+i(b-d)| \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(a+c)^2+(b+d)^2=(a-c)^2+(b-d)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2+c^2+2 a c+b^2+d^2+2 b d \\ & \quad=a^2+c^2-2 a c+b^2+d^2-2 b d \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 a c+4 b d=0 \\ & \quad a c=-b d \\ & \text { Now, } \frac{z_1}{z_2}=\frac{a+i b}{c+i d} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{(a+i b)(c-i d)}{(c+i d)(c-i d)} \\ & =\frac{(a c+b d)}{c^2+d^2}+i \frac{(b c-a d)}{c^2+d^2} \\ & =i\left[\frac{b c-a d}{c^2+d^2}\right], \quad[\because a c=-b d] \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \frac{z_1}{z_2}$$ is purely imaginary.

Take $$z_1=0$$ and $$z_2 \neq 0$$

Now, $$\left|\frac{z_1+z_2}{z_1-z_2}\right|=\left|\frac{z_2}{-z_2}\right|=|-1|=1$$

So, $$\frac{z_1}{z_2}=0$$

content: Let z1 and z2 be two non-zero complex numbers. Then

options.content: Principal value of arg(z<sub>1</sub>z<sub>2</sub>) may not be equal to Principal value of argz<sub>1</sub> + Principal value of arg z<sub>2</sub>
Principal value of arg(z<sub>1</sub>z<sub>2</sub>) = Principal value of argz<sub>1</sub> + Principal value of arg z<sub>2</sub>
Principal value of arg(z<sub>1</sub>/z<sub>2</sub>) = Principal value of argz<sub>1</sub> $$-$$ Principal value of arg z<sub>2</sub>
Principal value of arg(z<sub>1</sub>/z<sub>2</sub>) may not be argz<sub>1</sub> $$-$$ arg z<sub>2</sub>

explanation:
We use properties of argument of complex numbers.

(i) $$\arg \left( {{{{z_1}} \over {{z_2}}}} \right) = \arg ({z_1}) - \arg ({z_2}) + 2k\pi $$ (K = 0 or 1 or $$-$$ 1)

(ii) $$\arg ({z_1}{z_2}) = \arg ({z_1}) + \arg ({z_2}) + 2k\pi $$ (K = 0 or 1 or $$-$$ 1)

content: If $$\left| {z + i} \right| - \left| {z - 1} \right| = \left| z \right| - 2 = 0$$ for a complex number z, then z is equal to
explanation:
We have $$\left| {z + i} \right| - \left| {z - 1} \right| = \left| z \right| - 2$$

$$\left| z \right| - 2 \Rightarrow \left| z \right| = 2$$ represent a circle.


$$\left| {z + i} \right| - \left| {z - 1} \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left| {z + i} \right| = \left| {z - 1} \right|{x^2} + {(y + 1)^2} = {(x - 1)^2} + {y^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} + 2y + 1 = {x^2} - 2x + 1 + {y^2}$$

$$ = y = - x$$ ..... (i)


$$\therefore$$ $$\left| z \right| = 2 \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} = 4$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii)

$${x^2} + {x^2} = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} = 4x = \pm \sqrt 2 $$

and $$y = \mp \sqrt 2 $$

$$\therefore$$ $$z = x + iy$$

$$z = \pm \sqrt 2 \mp \sqrt {2i} $$

$$z = \pm \sqrt 2 (1 \mp i)$$

$$z = \sqrt 2 (1 - i)$$

and $$z = \sqrt 2 ( - 1 + i)$$

content: If $$\theta  \in R$$ and $${{1 - i\cos \theta } \over {1 + 2i\cos \theta }}$$ is real number, then $$\theta $$ will be (when I : Set of integers)
explanation:
Let $$Z = {{1 - i\cos \theta } \over {1 + 2i\cos \theta }}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\overline z = {{1 + i\cos \theta } \over {1 - 2i\cos \theta }}$$

Since, z is a real number, then $$z - \overline z = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{1 - i\cos \theta } \over {1 + 2i\cos \theta }} = {{1 + i\cos \theta } \over {1 - 2i\cos \theta }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow (1 - i\cos \theta )(1 - 2i\cos \theta ) = (1 + i\cos \theta )(1 + 2i\cos \theta )$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - 2icos\theta - icos\theta - 2co{s^2}\theta $$

$$ = 1 + 2i\cos \theta + i\cos \theta - 2{\cos ^2}\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow 6i\cos \theta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = (2n + 1){\pi \over 2},n \in I$$

content: The complex number z satisfying the equation | z $$-$$ 1 | = | z + 1 | = 1 is
explanation:
We have, | z $$-$$ i | = | z + 1 | = 1

Let, z = x + iy

$$ \therefore $$ | z $$-$$ i | = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ | x + iy $$-$$ 1 | = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ | x + (y $$-$$ 1) i | = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 + (y $$-$$ 1)2 = 1 .... (i)

Also, | z + 1 | = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ | x + iy + 1 | = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ | (x + 1) + iy | = 1

(x + 1)2 + y2 = 1 ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

x = y = 0 and x = $$-$$ 1, y = 1

$$ \therefore $$ z = 0, $$-$$1, y = 1

$$ \therefore $$ z = 0, $$-$$ 1 + i

content: If z = sin$$\theta$$ $$-$$ icos$$\theta$$, then for any integer n,
explanation:
Given, $$z = \sin \theta - i\cos \theta $$

$$ = \cos \left( {\theta - {\pi \over 2}} \right) + i\sin \left( {\theta - {\pi \over 2}} \right) = {e^{i\left( {\theta - {\pi \over 2}} \right)}}$$

Now,

$${z^n} = {e^{i\left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right)}} = \cos \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right) + i\sin \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{2^n}}} = {e^{i\left( {{{n\pi } \over 2} - n\theta } \right)}} = \cos \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right) - i\sin \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right)$$
$$ \therefore $$ $${z^n} + {1 \over {{z^n}}} = 2\cos \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right) = 2\cos \left( {{{n\pi } \over 2} - n\theta } \right)$$

and $${z^n} - {1 \over {{z^n}}} = 2i\sin \left( {n\theta - {{n\pi } \over 2}} \right)$$
